agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

### Agent supports the primitive (yes)

The scenario's **Primitive exercised** is a shell-escape mechanism â€” "Claude Code `!cmd`, or analogous." Aider implements the exact same primitive: the in-chat `/run` command runs a shell command locally (no LLM round-trip) and `!` is its documented shorthand alias. Per aider's in-chat command reference, `/run` is "Run a shell command and optionally add the output to the chat (alias: !)". So aider's `!echo hello` is a direct one-to-one analog of Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape this scenario is modeled on â€” not a loose stand-in. (https://aider.chat/docs/usage/commands.html)

### daemon_capability (full) â€” the daemon emits every spec-required observable

The **User-observable signal** the scenario asserts is exactly: "`ready â†’ working â†’ ready`; state does not stick in `working` after the shell command exits." The scenarios.json `verify` block adds `tool_calls_max: 0`. Both are met by the daemon AS IT EXISTS TODAY.

The transport is `FilesUnderCWD{Filename: ".aider.chat.history.md", RawLineParser}` (core/adapters/inbound/agents/aider/agent.go:45-51). For `/run echo hello`, aider writes a `#### /run echo hello` user-prompt line into the markdown history; the parser's `####` handler (parser.go:94-108) opens a turn â†’ `working`. ~1500ms after the file goes quiet, `IdleFlush` synthesizes `turn_done` (parser.go:216-229) â†’ `working â†’ ready`, clamped behind the SessionDetector ~5s working-refresh poll. Net lifecycle: `ready â†’ working â†’ ready`, never stuck in `working`, never `waiting`. That is precisely the asserted signal.

On the tool-count axis: the live aider 0.86.2 recording shows aider 0.86.2 does NOT write a `> Running <cmd>` blockquote for `/run` â€” it writes `> Add command output to the chat? (Y)es/(N)o` followed by `> Added N lines of output to the chat.`, which fall through the parser's generic ignored-blockquote branch (parser.go:126-133). So `runningRE` (`^>\s*Running\s+`, parser.go:69-70) never matches, `p.toolCall("Bash")` (parser.go:122-124) never fires, and ZERO Bash tool_results are emitted. The spec's `tool_calls_max: 0` therefore HOLDS for aider (zero tool calls observed) â€” for a different reason than claudecode (which deliberately skips its `<bash-input>`/`<bash-stdout>` wrappers), but the spec-required observable is satisfied either way.

Every Expected: signal the canonical spec requires â€” the state arc, no stick in working, no waiting, zero tool calls â€” is represented by the daemon today. There is no spec-required observable the daemon mis-handles, so this is `daemon_capability: full`, not `bug`. And the behavior leaves a clear trace in the watched Source (the `#### /run â€¦` line drives a real turn), so it is not `incapable`.

### Why this is `full` + a caveat, not `bug` (#476 re-derivation)

The old schema rated this `irrlicht_observes: partial`. That `partial` conflated two facts: (a) the spec-required state arc + zero-tool assertions are fully met (a `full` fact), and (b) a NON-spec property â€” irrlicht cannot tell a `/run` shell-escape apart from an ordinary user-prompt turn, since the escape surfaces only as the surrounding state arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event. Fact (b) is exactly the "feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant" caveat pattern: the canonical spec does NOT require a distinct shell-escape event (it asserts the arc + final ready + zero tools), so the indistinguishability is a caveat, NOT a `bug`. Per the authoring rule, caveats over downgrades â€” keep `daemon_capability: full` and surface the gap as a yellow callout.

### driver_capability (ready)

The recipe's steps are `slash` (`/run echo hello`), `sleep`, `send` (the final normal prompt), `wait_turn`, `sleep`. The aider interactive driver (scripts/drive-aider-interactive.sh) implements send / slash / wait_turn / interrupt / sleep. `/run echo hello` is an inline-arg slash (delivered verbatim via tmux send-keys -l), NOT an arrow-key picker, so no `keys` step is needed. Every step type the recipe needs exists â†’ `driver_capability: ready`. None of the driver's known gaps (restart, reset_session, resume, start_session, exit_clean, sigkill, keys, session, fork) is on this recipe's path.

### Capability key

No dedicated `shell_escape` key exists in any adapter's capabilities.json. The `/run`/`!` surface is gated by `slash_commands: true`; the observation channel would conventionally be `tool_calls: true`, but the live aider 0.86.2 recording shows the escape emits NO tool call (no `> Running` line â†’ runningRE never fires), so the escape is observed via the surrounding `ready â†’ working â†’ ready` state arc rather than through `tool_calls`. Both keys are `true` for aider (capabilities.json:18,11).

## Routing

supports=yes, daemon=full, driver=ready â†’ route: record. The recorded spec asserts the state arc + final `ready` + zero tool calls / no tool_result/Bash marker.
caveats: FEATURE-INVISIBLE-BUT-SPEC-COMPLIANT: aider 0.86.2's `/run` writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output`, NOT a `> Running <cmd>` line, so runningRE (parser.go:69-70) never fires and zero Bash tool_results are emitted. irrlicht therefore cannot distinguish the shell-escape from an ordinary user-prompt turn â€” it is observed only as the readyâ†’workingâ†’ready arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event. The canonical spec does not require a distinct event, so this is a caveat, not a daemon defect.

The scenario's claudecode-shaped verify.tool_calls_max:0 DOES hold for aider 0.86.2 (zero tool calls observed), but for a different reason than claudecode: aider writes no parseable tool line at all, vs claudecode deliberately skipping its <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrappers.

Workingâ†’ready latency is idle-driven (IdleFlush after ~1500ms, clamped behind the SessionDetector ~5s working-refresh poll), so the ready transition trails the shell command's exit by several seconds rather than firing on the command's completion line.

TEARDOWN-RACE: the final workingâ†’ready IdleFlush settle must land before the tmux kill or the recording ends mid-working and the final ready + process_exited are lost. The recipe uses a â‰Ą12s trailing sleep for this reason; in practice the FINAL turn's settle still races process death (reproduced across 4 recordings, sleeps 12/18/20s) â€” the assessed latency carve-out, marked known_failing in expected.jsonl. This is a latency artifact, not a spec-required observable the daemon mis-handles.

sources.note: In-chat commands: '/run â€” Run a shell command and optionally add the output to the chat (alias: !)'. Confirms aider's !cmd shell-escape is the direct analog of Claude Code's !cmd. Anchors agent_supports: yes.

The `####` user-prompt handler (L94-108) opens the turn â†’ working; IdleFlush turn_done (L216-229) settles â†’ ready â€” the full readyâ†’workingâ†’ready arc the spec asserts. runningRE ^>\s*Running\s+ (L69-70) â†’ toolCall("Bash") (L122-124) fires ONLY for a literal `> Running` line; aider 0.86.2's `/run` writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output`, which fall through the generic ignored-blockquote branch (L126-133), so no Bash tool_result is emitted (zero tool calls). Grounds daemon_capability: full + the feature-invisible caveat.

TestParser_ToolCall_RunningShell (L162-172): a LITERAL '> Running echo hello' line produces exactly one Bash ToolUse â€” pins the runningREâ†’Bash path. aider 0.86.2 does NOT write that line for `/run` (it writes the `> Add command outputâ€¦` confirmation pair), so this path is not exercised by a real `/run` escape; the live recording shows zero tool calls.

Source = FilesUnderCWD{.aider.chat.history.md, RawLineParser} (L45-51) â€” the markdown transcript that carries the `#### /run â€¦` line the parser keys on. One Parser per process; no PendingContributor/SubagentCounter. Confirms the trace exists in the watched Source (not incapable).

No shell_escape key; slash_commands:true (L19) gates /run, tool_calls:true (L11) is the conventional observation channel but the /run escape emits no tool call. Maps the 'shell-escape mechanism' primitive to existing keys (Step 4 confirmation).

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a model out-of-band (IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL plus the matching provider key/endpoint â€” e.g. a local LM Studio / OpenAI-compatible server). Headless aider with no reachable provider hangs on the model-auth prompt; the live REPL needs a model that actually answers the final normal prompt.
tmux available â€” the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so the shell-escape and the final prompt can both be issued to one long-lived session.
irrlichd is recording â€” either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record.

OBSERVES:PARTIAL (per assessment.json â€” irrlicht_observes: partial): aider's `/run <cmd>` (alias `!<cmd>`) is the direct analog of Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape (agent_supports: yes), but irrlicht does NOT surface it as anything distinct from an ordinary turn. OBSERVABILITY CORRECTION (live aider 0.86.2 recording, supersedes the earlier doc-based `> Running`-fold prediction): aider 0.86.2's `/run` does NOT write a `> Running <cmd>` line â€” it writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output` confirmation blockquotes, so runningRE never fires and ZERO Bash tool_results are emitted. The claudecode-shaped top-level verify.tool_calls_max:0 therefore DOES hold for aider 0.86.2 (zero tool calls), but for a different reason than claudecode. This cell asserts the readyâ†’workingâ†’ready arc + final ready and asserts NO tool_result/Bash marker â€” the escape is observed only as the state arc; the absence of a distinct shell-escape event is the documented partial scope, not a defect.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in the transcript before sending the first step.

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears. There is no transcript-UUID rollout session â€” the whole arc lives on the one proc-<PID> row.

The shell-escape is issued via a `slash` step typing the literal text `/run echo hello` + Enter (the aider driver handles `slash` identically to `send` via tmux send-keys -l, so the inline argument is delivered verbatim; `/run` is an inline-arg slash, NOT an arrow-key picker, so no `keys` step is needed). `/run echo hello` writes a `#### /run echo hello` user-prompt line (opens a turn â†’ working) then aider 0.86.2 writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output` confirmation blockquotes â€” NOT a `> Running` line â€” so runningRE never fires and NO Bash tool_result is emitted (OBSERVES:PARTIAL correction). NO wait_turn follows the escape â€” it produces no `> Tokens:` line, so wait_turn would time out; a trailing sleep lets IdleFlush settle workingâ†’ready instead.

aider has NO turn-end transcript marker â€” end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after 1.5s quiet, surfaced as workingâ†’ready behind the daemon's ~5s SessionDetector working-refresh poll (so the workingâ†’ready settle trails the command exit by several seconds; this is why the post-escape sleep is â‰Ą7s).
After the final wait_turn + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once â€” aider exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the session.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession (proc-<PID>) event in ready within ~2s of REPL launch (aider's transcript is lazy, so the pre-session is the launch-time observable)

the /run echo hello shell-escape drives a readyâ†’workingâ†’ready arc on the bindable session: the `#### /run echo hello` line opens the turn (â†’working), aider runs the command locally, and IdleFlush settles the turn back to ready (~5s effective latency) â€” the escape does NOT stick in working and never enters waiting

NO-TOOL_RESULT (the observes:partial scope, observability correction vs the earlier doc-based prediction): aider 0.86.2 emits NO Bash tool_result for the `/run` escape. It writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output`, NOT a `> Running` line, so runningRE never matches and zero tool calls are recorded. The escape is observed only as the readyâ†’workingâ†’ready state arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event. The top-level verify.tool_calls_max:0 happens to hold (zero tool calls) but for a different reason than claudecode; this is documented scope, not drift, and NO tool_result/Bash marker is asserted.
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the bindable aider session_id

after the normal user prompt, events.jsonl shows a second readyâ†’workingâ†’ready arc on the SAME session_id â€” proves the session is still alive and a real prompt still drives state after the shell-escape
no state_transition to waiting appears for the session at any point
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the session at REPL teardown (one long-lived session); final session state is ready
recipe.notes: aider vs claudecode/codex/pi for this scenario: aider's `/run <cmd>` (documented alias `!<cmd>`) runs a shell command locally with no LLM round-trip â€” the direct analog of Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape (assessment.json agent_supports: yes). The recipe is templated from the claudecode shell-escape recipe but diverges on the observation shape (assessment.json irrlicht_observes: PARTIAL). claudecode's parser deliberately SKIPS the <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrappers so the escape synthesizes NO turn and state stays ready throughout (its top-level verify.tool_calls_max:0 holds). OBSERVABILITY CORRECTION (live aider 0.86.2 recording â€” supersedes the earlier doc-based prediction that aider folds the escape into a Bash tool_result): aider 0.86.2's `/run` does NOT write a `> Running <cmd>` blockquote, so runningRE (^>\\s*Running\\s+, parser.go:69-70) never matches and p.toolCall("Bash") (parser.go:122-124) never fires â€” ZERO Bash tool_results are emitted. aider 0.86.2 instead writes `> Add command output to the chat?` + `> Added N lines of output` confirmation blockquotes, which fall through to the generic ignored-blockquote branch. (parser_test.go TestParser_ToolCall_RunningShell still pins the runningREâ†’Bash path for a LITERAL `> Running` line, but `/run` never produces that line.) What still holds: the `####` user-prompt line aider writes for `/run` opens a real turn (â†’working), and ~1500ms later IdleFlush synthesizes turn_done (workingâ†’ready, clamped behind the SessionDetector ~5s poll). So the observable is a genuine readyâ†’workingâ†’ready arc (NOT claudecode's stay-in-ready) with NO tool call â€” the escape is observed only as that arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event; that indistinguishability is exactly why the cell is partial. verify.tool_calls_max:0 happens to hold for aider 0.86.2 (zero tool calls), but for a different reason than claudecode. RECIPE SHAPE: the escape comes FIRST (via `slash` â€” the aider interactive driver supports send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep but NOT keys; `/run echo hello` is an inline-arg slash so `slash` delivers it verbatim, no `keys` gap), with NO wait_turn (it produces no `> Tokens:` line) followed by a â‰Ą7s sleep so IdleFlush's workingâ†’ready lands before the next prompt re-enters working; then a normal `send`+`wait_turn` proves the session is still alive and a real prompt still drives the arc; a â‰Ą12s trailing sleep holds the recording open past the final IdleFlush settle so the tmux kill doesn't race the final workingâ†’ready emission + process_exited (TEARDOWN RACE: an 8s tail let the kill land mid-working and lost the final ready + process_exited; â‰Ą12s gives the ~5s SessionDetector poll + IdleFlush full room to settle). SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL surfaces a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch; the whole arc lives on that one proc-<PID> row (no transcript-UUID rollout for FilesUnderCWD). In expected.jsonl, escape_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin (matcher picks the FIRST working â€” the pre-session never goes working), and pid_bind / escape_end / final_turn_start / final_turn_end / teardown pin via same_session_as so a co-occurring pre-session can never satisfy them. Between runs: the shell command stdout, exact final-turn assistant wording, per-turn token counts, session UUID, PID and timestamps differ. If it broke, look at core/adapters/inbound/agents/aider/parser.go (the `####`â†’turn-open at parser.go:94-108 and the IdleFlush turn_done at parser.go:216-229) and whether an aider update changed how `/run` / `!` escapes are written into .aider.chat.history.md (e.g. a return of the `> Running` line, which would re-introduce a Bash tool_result). KNOWN FAILING (5/7, live aider 0.86.2): the load-bearing escape arc (escape_startâ†’escape_end: the /run shell-escape does NOT stick in working) + session_birth/pid_bind/final_turn_start all PASS, but final_turn_end + teardown FAIL â€” aider does not reliably emit the FINAL turn's workingâ†’ready settle before the tmux kill. When the final turn's last transcript write coincides with the working transition (no later fswatcher activity to re-arm the tailer), the SessionDetector refresh-ticker IdleFlush (~5s poll) races process death and the settle is lost. Reproduced across 4 recordings (trailing sleeps 12/18/20s; both file-editing and plain-reply final prompts). This is the assessed irrlicht_observes:partial latency carve-out, not a daemon regression and not recipe-fixable; marked known_failing in expected.jsonl. PROMPT NOTE: the final prompt is "Reply with exactly the word: ok" (mirrors basic-turn), but gpt-5-nano sometimes still interprets the word as a filename and edits a file â€” harmless to the arc; the teardown gap is independent of whether the final turn edits.
